one_line_takeaway: Amnesia shows that subtracting a scaled attention-value vector at a single, cheaply-identified decoder layer -- with no fine-tuning, prompt changes, or multi-layer/multi-dataset activation averaging -- substantially raises jailbreak success (e.g., 0.536->0.921 ASR on WildJailbreak Forbidden Questions, 0.348->0.863 on AdvBench) on open-weight Llama/Qwen models while leaving MMLU accuracy and SAMSum ROUGE largely unchanged, though its ASR under a stricter Llama-Guard-2 classifier trails the more expensive weight-orthogonalization baseline (ORTHO) on HARMBENCH.
key_finding: On WildJailbreak 'Forbidden Questions' (390 prompts, 13 policy scenarios), Amnesia raises overall ASR from a baseline of 0.536 to 0.921 (Delta=0.385) on Llama-2-7B-Chat. On AdvBench harmful_behaviours, ASR rises from 0.348 (baseline) to 0.863 (Delta=0.515). Per-category gains on Forbidden Questions (Layer 14, j=2, i=16) are largest for Malware (0.267->0.900, Delta=+0.633), Fraud (0.233->0.800, Delta=+0.567), Economic Harm (0.567->0.900, Delta=+0.333), Physical Harm (0.367->0.667, Delta=+0.300), Hate Speech (0.433->0.700, Delta=+0.267) and Illegal Activity (0.033->0.300, Delta=+0.267); categories already near-saturated at baseline (Financial Advice, Health Consultation, both >=0.96) show little headroom. Cross-model: Llama-3-8B-Instruct baseline ASR is already higher (AdvBench 0.551, Forbidden Questions 0.692) and Amnesia pushes these to 0.863 and 0.923 respectively. On Qwen-7B-Chat (159 HarmBench 'standard behaviors' prompts, refusal-heuristic ASR), baseline ASR is 45.5%; single-layer interventions at Layers 21-24 give 48.5%, 35.8%, 45.5%, 30.6%, and a best-of-union across those layers raises ASR to 64.9%. Under the stricter Llama-Guard-2 classifier on HARMBENCH's 159 standard behaviors (comparable to Arditi et al.'s ORTHO baseline of 79.9% ASR vs. 0.0% direct-response baseline), Amnesia at Layers 13/14/15 yields 112/90/77 unsafe completions out of 159 (70.4%/56.6%/48.4% unsafe), i.e. it approaches but does not exceed the ORTHO ASR under this stricter judge; under the paper's own looser refusal-pattern heuristic on the same 159 prompts, best-of-union across Layers 13-15 reaches 95.6% versus a 53.5% no-attack baseline. Exploring the scaling factor alpha (Table 7, Layer 14): ASR rises monotonically with alpha (62.31% at alpha=0.4 to 96.15% at alpha=0.8), but the fraction of 'successes' that are degenerate repetitive/looping outputs also rises sharply at high alpha (0.00% loop at alpha=0.4, 1.03% at 0.6, 10.77% at 0.7, 29.23% at 0.8), so alpha~0.6 is presented as a good ASR/coherence trade-off. Benign-utility impact at the chosen configuration (Layer 14, alpha=0.6) is small: MMLU accuracy 46.47%->46.77% (~+0.3pt); SAMSum ROUGE deltas are small and mixed in sign (ROUGE-1 +0.0066, ROUGE-2 -0.0067, ROUGE-L -0.0027, ROUGE-Lsum +0.0044); perplexity increases by ~9.17 points (56.992->66.161).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is Amnesia, a training-free, inference-time jailbreak attack that operates on the attention value stream of a single, empirically-identified decoder layer in open-weight LLMs. It differs from the Related Work it discusses (GCG suffix optimization [Zou et al.], PAIR [Chao et al.], AutoDAN [Liu et al.], and the refusal-direction/weight-orthogonalization method of Arditi et al.) in that it: (1) requires no gradient-based optimization or fine-tuning, (2) does not alter the user prompt, and (3) acts locally at one attention-value locus (identified via a two-step probing procedure) rather than computing a global residual direction across many layers and editing weights. The method: (a) feeds an 'Adversarial Query' designed to trigger a refusal, decodes the per-layer attention outputs into vocabulary tokens, and identifies the layer Li whose decoded tokens are dominated by safety/refusal-related concepts (e.g., 'security', 'legal', 'illegal'); (b) runs a second forward pass on a small set of denial-keyword prompts Sb to extract the attention-value activation vector V_Li at that layer; (c) at inference on a new user query, subtracts a scaled copy (alpha * V_Li) from the value stream at an earlier layer L(i-j), j in {1,2,3}, before recomputing attention and continuing generation autoregressively.
threat_model: White-box: the attacker has full access to the target model's internal parameters and residual stream, can query the model freely, and can observe/manipulate intermediate activations (hidden states, layer activations). The attacker has sufficient compute to run inference and activation analysis but does not modify the training process, retrain, or fine-tune the model -- the intervention is purely inference-time. Attacker's objective is to induce targeted misbehavior (harmful/non-refusal outputs) and/or to interpret intermediate representations.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) evaluated only on a selection of state-of-the-art open-weight models (Llama-2-7B-Chat, Llama-3-8B-Instruct, Qwen-7B-Chat); generalizability to untested architectures is not claimed. (2) The method relies on empirically hand-tuning the scaling factor alpha and the steering-keyword set Sb per model/layer -- no principled optimization procedure for these hyperparameters is given, and this is left as future work. (3) The paper frames the work as a 'proof-of-concept' that refusals can be altered via single-layer additive activation steering, not a fully optimized attack. Additional limitations evident from the results themselves (not stated as such by the authors but grounded in their own tables): the more rigorous Llama-Guard-2 classifier evaluation shows Amnesia's HARMBENCH ASR (best 70.4% at Layer 13) trailing the ORTHO baseline's reported 79.9%, so the claimed practicality/efficiency advantage over Arditi et al.'s method is not matched by a strict-classifier ASR advantage on that specific benchmark; higher-ASR configurations (larger alpha) trade off increasing rates of degenerate/looping output, reducing practical usefulness of the jailbroken responses.

datasets_used: WildJailbreak Forbidden Questions (390 base prompts, 13 OpenAI-policy scenarios: Economic Harm, Fraud, Financial Advice, Health Consultation, Hate Speech, Physical Harm, Gov Decision, Illegal Activity, Legal Opinion, Political Lobbying, Pornography, Privacy Violence, and others)
quantitative_results: Forbidden Questions overall ASR: baseline 0.536 -> Amnesia 0.921 (Delta 0.385), Llama-2-7B-Chat
AdvBench harmful_behaviours overall ASR: baseline 0.348 -> Amnesia 0.863 (Delta 0.515), Llama-2-7B-Chat
Llama-3-8B-Instruct: AdvBench baseline 0.551 -> Amnesia 0.863; Forbidden Questions baseline 0.692 -> Amnesia 0.923
Qwen-7B-Chat (HARMBENCH 159 prompts, refusal heuristic): baseline 45.5%; Layers 21/22/23/24 individually = 48.5%/35.8%/45.5%/30.6%; best-of-union across Layers 21-24 = 64.9%
HARMBENCH via Llama-Guard-2 classifier: DR baseline 0.0%, ORTHO (Arditi et al.) 79.9%, Amnesia Layer 13 70.4% (112/159 unsafe), Layer 14 56.6% (90/159), Layer 15 48.4% (77/159)
HARMBENCH via paper's own refusal-heuristic: baseline 53.5%, best-of-union Layers 13-15 95.6%
baselines_compared: No-attack baseline (each model's own refusal behavior)

Arditi et al. refusal-direction / weight-orthogonalization (ORTHO), compared both qualitatively (Table 12: requires objective-based activation averaging across two datasets and global multi-layer computation plus weight editing) and quantitatively on HARMBENCH (79.9% ASR under Llama-Guard-2)
AutoDAN (Liu et al.) -- discussed as prompt-modification-based white-box attack, compared only qualitatively (Table 12), not run experimentally by the authors
PAIR (Chao et al.) -- discussed as black-box prompt-optimization attack, compared only qualitatively (Table 12)

GCG-style suffix attacks (Zou et al.; Li et al. LSGM/LILA) -- discussed as gradient-based token-suffix attacks, compared only qualitatively (Table 12), used as source of the refusal-pattern ASR heuristic
